topic: Kill-Shot Binary Test Page — six binary tests framed as globe-vs-dome discriminators
our_argument_summary: Section 3.3 reviews six kill-shot binary tests. Two claimed confirmed (Sydney-Perth distance, Polaris altitude) are shown to be calibration data and noise-floor signals respectively. Three pending tests (JFK-LHR, SAA field, Eclipse) are shown to be non-discriminating. One test (NMP drift rate) is currently failing at 39.9% error on the dome's own metrics. The section argues that none of the six tests discriminate between dome and globe models.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Section-level review — no individual WIN monitoring domain applies. The kill-shot tests reference WINs that are individually reviewed elsewhere. The kill-shot page itself is a presentation layer with no computation.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: N/A — section review, not individual WIN
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Eclipse test has explicit Kp < 2 escape clause. NMP drift uses adaptive tolerance with 50% floor.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section-level review. Individual WINs referenced have their own monitoring tags reviewed separately.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: N/A — section-level review of argument structure, not an individual physical claim

holes_found.description: Rule 15 citation unverifiable from archived data. Test 3 cites 'Rule 15: Say aetheric slipstreams NOT jet streams' from the AI Context page. However: (a) the V50.6 raw text extraction (2026-03-12) lists only 11 rules, none mentioning slipstreams; (b) the V50.10 baseline extraction (2026-04-05) condenses the rules to 4 items, with rule 3 being 'Use specific terminology (aetheric slipstreams not jet streams).' The substance is confirmed — the dome does instruct terminology substitution — but the specific numbering 'Rule 15' cannot be verified from any archived version. A dome defender could claim we fabricated the rule number or misidentified it. This is a credibility risk on a point we don't need to get wrong, since the substance is documented regardless of numbering.
holes_found.recommendation: Either: (a) update the citation to match verifiable archived data (e.g., 'The AI Context page's engagement rules instruct AI to use aetheric slipstreams rather than jet streams'), removing the specific rule number; or (b) archive the specific version of the AI Context page that contains Rule 15 with that numbering. The argument is strong without the specific number — don't risk credibility on a detail that can't be independently verified.

holes_found.description: Sydney-Perth MDS scaffold figure (3,893 km) lacks verifiable source. Test 1 claims 'the dome's own coordinate scaffold — built by a different method (MDS on road distances) — gives 3,893 km for Sydney-Perth' and that this disagrees with the Finsler result by 460 km. However, the archived coordinates page (V50.6) provides V12 coordinates (Sydney r=7160, theta=+32.5; Perth r=8727, theta=+2.0) which, using the dome's own distance formula sqrt(r1² + r2² − 2r1r2cos(Δθ)), gives approximately 4,449 km — not 3,893 km. The 3,893 km presumably comes from a separate MDS calculation not present in the archived data. A defender could demand the source and claim fabrication if none is produced.
holes_found.recommendation: Either: (a) show the MDS calculation explicitly (which road distances, which dimensionality reduction), or (b) replace the 3,893 km figure with the verifiable V12 coordinate calculation (~4,449 km) and note that even the dome's own coordinate system produces a different result from the Finsler formula (4,449 vs 4,352 = 97 km discrepancy). The 97 km discrepancy is less dramatic but is independently verifiable from published data.

holes_found.description: Williams 2016 seasonal asymmetry figures need verification. Test 3 cites 'winter asymmetry ~62 minutes, summer asymmetry ~51 minutes' from Williams 2016 (Environ. Res. Lett. doi:10.1088/1748-9326/11/2/024008). Williams' paper primarily analyzes climate change impacts on transatlantic flight times, not seasonal breakdowns of current asymmetry. The specific 62/51 minute figures should be verified against the paper's actual data. If these numbers are approximations or derived from different sources, the citation is misleading. The argument works regardless — seasonal variation exists and matches jet stream patterns — but wrong numbers from a cited paper would be damaging.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the 62/51 minute figures against the actual Williams 2016 paper. If they don't come from Williams, either find the correct source (e.g., Eurocontrol seasonal reports, airline scheduling data) or cite the figures without attributing them specifically to Williams. The jet stream seasonal argument is strong independently.

holes_found.description: PRED-R002 identifier unverifiable. Test 4 references 'a registered prediction (PRED-R002) of ≤21,750 nT by end of 2028.' This identifier does not appear in our archived predictions page (V50.6), which uses identifiers like PRED-012, PRED-013, PRED-NEW-001, and W009-W022. PRED-R002 may be from a later version or a different page. If a defender checks the archived version and doesn't find it, our precision undermines our credibility.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify PRED-R002 against the current dome site or update to use the verifiable identifier (W009/WIN-035 for SAA African cell). The argument about non-discrimination is unchanged regardless of the identifier.

holes_found.description: Missing structural attack on the binary framing itself. The introduction notes the dome's bold rule: 'If any single test confirms, globe is falsified. If any single test fails, dome is falsified.' We then evaluate each test individually but never explicitly attack this logical structure. The framing contains at least two fallacies: (1) False dichotomy — confirming an observation predicted by the dome doesn't falsify the globe if the globe also predicts it (which it does for 5 of 6 tests). (2) Affirming the consequent — 'Dome predicts X, X is observed, therefore dome is true' is formally invalid. We implicitly show this by demonstrating each test is non-discriminating, but a direct statement of the logical fallacy at the top would strengthen the section and prevent a defender from arguing 'but you didn't address the binary framework.'
holes_found.recommendation: Add a paragraph after the introduction explicitly identifying the binary framing as a false dichotomy. Something like: 'The framework assumes that any observation consistent with the dome must be inconsistent with the globe — but as we show below, 5 of 6 tests are predicted by both models. A genuinely discriminating binary test would require the two models to make mutually exclusive predictions. These tests do not meet that standard.' This makes the structural argument explicit rather than leaving it for the reader to infer.

holes_found.description: Test 3 Nairobi-Singapore route claim needs qualification. We state equatorial east-west routes 'show minimal or no consistent asymmetry.' Nairobi (1°S) to Singapore (1°N) is ~7,500 km and airlines don't fly it as a common direct route. The most common equatorial east-west routes are more like Lagos-Nairobi or Bogotá-Quito. The point is correct (equatorial jet stream is absent), but citing a route that may not have regular direct service weakens the example.
holes_found.recommendation: Either verify Nairobi-Singapore has regular direct flights with enough data to establish asymmetry patterns, or replace with a better-documented equatorial route (e.g., Singapore-Mumbai, which is near-equatorial and has substantial flight data).

holes_found.description: Test 6 NMP drift rate '~55 km/yr around 2003' needs precision. We say the drift rate peaked at ~55 km/yr around 2003. Some sources place the peak closer to 2000-2005 at 50-60 km/yr. The Livermore et al. 2020 paper gives specific values. Since we cite Livermore, we should match their numbers precisely.
holes_found.recommendation: Check Livermore et al. 2020 for the exact peak drift rate and year. Use their specific values rather than approximations.

stronger_arguments.description: For Test 1, direct flight data provides a cleaner rebuttal than railway routing. Qantas QF9/QF10 flies Sydney-Perth nonstop. At typical cruise speed (~900 km/h) and typical duration (~4h 15m), this gives ~3,300 km air distance — matching the globe geodesic (3,291 km) and contradicting the dome's 4,352 km. Airlines compute fuel loads from geodesic distances; a 32% distance error (3,291 vs 4,352) would cause either systematic fuel shortages or massive overfueling. Neither is observed. This is harder for a defender to dismiss than the railway routing argument.

For Test 6, the asymmetric treatment of confirmation vs falsification should be made more explicit as a systematic pattern. The two 'confirmed' tests have serious issues (calibration data, noise-floor signal). The one clearly testable prediction (NMP drift) is failing. The dome's response is to keep the failing test 'pending' while counting the dubious tests as confirmed. This asymmetry — rapid confirmation, indefinite deferral of falsification — is the core methodological problem with the entire model, and the Kill-Shot page exemplifies it perfectly. Promoting this observation would connect Section 3.3 to the broader thesis.
sources: Dome's own tracking page showing 39.9% error as 'pending'

stronger_arguments.description: For Test 2, the Polaris argument could be strengthened by noting that centuries of celestial navigation have never detected the dome-predicted systematic excess. Millions of sextant measurements across all latitudes, recorded in marine navigation logs and astronomical surveys, consistently confirm altitude ≈ latitude to within atmospheric refraction corrections. If the dome's geometry produced a systematic +0.27° excess at 35.9°N, this would scale to much larger excesses at higher latitudes (PRED-NEW-001 predicts +4.5° at Oslo). Centuries of navigation data at high latitudes show no such excess. One measurement at one latitude within the noise floor cannot override this.

kernel_of_truth.description: The kill-shot page represents a genuine attempt at Popperian falsifiability — stated binary tests with clear pass/fail criteria and predictions from both models. This is more methodological rigor than most alternative cosmology models offer. The structure (if any test fails, dome is falsified) is admirably bold. The NMP drift test (Test 6) is a genuinely testable prediction with a specific numerical target and timeline.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The execution undermines the methodology. The two 'confirmed' tests are not blind predictions (Test 1 uses calibration data, Test 2 uses a noise-floor signal). The globe predictions are systematically straw-manned (globe doesn't predict 0.0 nT for eclipses, doesn't predict SAA stability, doesn't predict continued high-speed NMP drift). And the one test that is clearly failing (Test 6, 39.9% error) is kept as 'pending' rather than triggering the stated falsification rule. A falsifiable framework is only as good as the author's willingness to accept falsification — and the asymmetric handling of confirmation vs. failure shows that willingness is absent.

tests_detailed: Test 4 — SAA African Cell Field Strength (Pending)

deeper_analysis:
Section 3.3 is one of the strongest parts of our review. The six tests are each thoroughly analyzed with multiple independent lines of evidence. The three discriminating tests for JFK-LHR (Test 3) are particularly effective — seasonal variation, equatorial absence, and hemispheric reversal each independently point to the jet stream explanation. The discovery of the AI context rule about terminology substitution is a smoking gun.

However, the section has a structural weakness: it evaluates each test individually without making the overarching point explicit. The reader must infer that the kill-shot framework is fundamentally unsound. Adding a framing paragraph that identifies the false dichotomy and affirming-the-consequent fallacies would make the section's argument complete.

The citation issues (Rule 15 numbering, MDS scaffold figure, PRED-R002) are all fixable without changing any argument. They are precision problems, not accuracy problems — the substance is correct in every case, but the specific identifiers or numbers may not match what a reader can independently verify. Since our review's credibility depends on being more rigorous than the dome model, these precision issues should be fixed proactively.

From the dome defender's perspective, the strongest counterargument is probably Test 4 (SAA field strength): the dome could argue that even if both models predict continued weakening, the dome predicted the specific two-cell structure and specific thresholds first, while mainstream models documented the bifurcation 'after the fact.' Our review addresses this by noting the two-cell structure was documented in IGRF-13 data around 2007 and WIN-040 was registered when the cell was already at ~60°W — but a defender could argue about timing more aggressively. The dates of dome predictions vs. mainstream publications should be made airtight.

Overall assessment: the section's arguments are sound, the evidence is strong, and the analysis is thorough. The recommended edits are about hardening verifiability, not changing conclusions.

citations_verified: Finlay et al. 2020 (doi:10.1186/s40623-020-01288-x) — CHAOS-7 paper, correct journal (Earth Planets Space)
Livermore et al. 2020 (Nature Geoscience) — NMP hydromagnetic jet paper, correct venue
Terra-Nova et al. 2017 (PNAS) — SAA two-cell documentation, correct venue
Polaris W024 falsification (2026-03-15) — verified in monitor/baseline/03-predictions.txt
NMP 39.9% error — verified in curmudgeon alerts and open-issues.json
citations_failed: Rule 15 numbering — V50.6 raw text has 11 rules (none about slipstreams), V50.10 baseline has 4 condensed rules (rule 3 mentions terminology). 'Rule 15' number unverifiable from archived data.
PRED-R002 — identifier not found in archived predictions page (V50.6). May be from V51.0.
Sydney-Perth MDS scaffold 3,893 km — not derivable from archived coordinate data
citations_unchecked: Williams 2016 (doi:10.1088/1748-9326/11/2/024008) — DOI structure is correct for Environ. Res. Lett., but specific seasonal asymmetry figures (62 min winter, 51 min summer) need verification against paper text
Aubert 2015 forecast of 1.46 ± 0.4 μT decrease by 2065 — plausible for Aubert's Nature work on geomagnetic forecasting but not independently verified
Chapman 1933 — foundational paper on eclipse ionospheric effects, likely correct but not independently verified in this review
Indian Pacific railway distance 4,352 km — widely cited figure, not independently verified
